rows.collected_data: Patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) including Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS-v1.1) for symptom severity; WHO-5 Well-Being Index; Barratt Impulsiveness Scale (BIS-11); adherence logs; session completion rates; quality of life assessments.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reduction in ADHD symptoms (primary: ASRS score decrease 11.6% points in intervention vs. ~4.4 in control; p<0.001; effect size 0.65); improved impulsivity (BIS-11 ~15% reduction; p<0.01) and well-being (WHO-5 increase ~10 points; p<0.05); high adherence (~75%); clinically meaningful improvements exceeding MCID for ASRS (>9 points).

rows.collected_data: Guided: A seven-module program with written feedback provided by trained and supervised eCoaches, totaling approximately 1.75 hours of support per participant. Unguided: The identical seven-module program, differing only by the absence of human support. CG: Participants received the offer to use the ACTonPainunguided intervention after the final 6-month assessment. Crucially, all three arms maintained unrestricted access to care-as-usual.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The guided ACTonPain group showed significantly less pain interference than the control group in the ITT analysis (p = 0.01), with a moderate effect size at T1 and T2 (d = 0.58 respectively), corresponding to a number needed to treat (NNT) of 3.14 for both time points. Participants in the guided ACTonPain group also indicated higher pain acceptance (T1: d = 0.59; T2: d = 0.76). The unguided ACTonPain group showed to be significantly less depressed in comparison to the control group at at time T2 (d = 0.50). No significant differences with respect to effectiveness were found between the two ACTonPain groups (p>0.05).

rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention: A minimally guided online self-help program based on Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), specifically systematic behavioural activation. Included six core sessions, two optional sessions, and an optional booster session.  Control: An online psychoeducation program on depression.  Participants were adults with Type 1 or Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus and elevated depressive symptoms (CES-D > 22).
rows.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Level of depression (assessed by the German version of the Center for Epidemiological Studies Depression Scale - CES-D).  Secondary Outcomes: Diabetes-specific emotional distress, glycaemic control, self-management behaviour, satisfaction, and costs.  Utility measures for economic evaluation: QALYs (specific instrument not detailed in the economic summary, but standard QALY calculation was used)
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Effectiveness: GET.ON M.E.D. was effective in reducing symptoms of depression over 2 and 6 months compared to the active control group.  Sustained effect: 45% of participants showed no clinically relevant symptoms at 6 months post-randomization. Economic: The intervention has a high probability of being cost-effective compared with the active psychoeducation control group, especially from a cost-effectiveness perspective.  Incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) and incremental cost-utility ratio (ICUR) were calculated using non-parametric bootstrap resampling techniques (2500 replications) over a 6-month period.

rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The IG received a personal information session, followed by a questionnaire. The CG filled out the questionnaire immediately.
rows.collected_data: The present study examined for the first time diabetes patients’ acceptance of Internet-based depression interventions for diabetes patients. Moreover, the impact of an acceptance facilitating intervention (AFI) consisting of an informational session on diabetes patients’ acceptance of IBIs for depression was examined
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Only 11.6% of the participants who showed interest in receiving some form of psychological or behavioral intervention expressed their serious interest in an IBI, while 51.8% negated being interested in this form of intervention. Compared to the other delivery options, IBIs were the least favored intervention approach. Thus, while IBIs are effective for the treatment of depression  and attrition rates of IBI participants are rather low for those who started an IBI , the reach of IBIs in the whole target population seems very limited. Participants of the study conducted by Gun et al. reported that the likelihood to take up an IBI in the future could be facilitated by providing them with information on the effectiveness, accessibility and safety of IBIs; a suggestion that we experimentally tested in the present study.

rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1:Intervention group: Patients treated with the Rise-uP intervention (therapy navigator, telemedicine, back app)Arm 2:Control group: Patients receiving routine treatment (standard care)
rows.collected_data: ssessments of pain, psychological state, functional capacity, and well-being (patient-reported outcome measures; PROMs) were collected at baseline, and at 3-, 6-, and 12-months follow-up intervals. Health insurance partners AOK, DAK, and BARMER provided individual healthcare cost data. An artificial intelligence (AI)-driven behavioral tracking analysis identified distinct app usage clusters that presented all with about the same clinical outcome. Patient satisfaction (patient-reported experience measures; PREMs) was captured at the end of the trial.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis demonstrated that the Rise-uP group experienced significantly greater pain reduction at 12 months compared to the control group (IG: − 46% vs CG: − 24%; p < 0.001) with only the Rise-uP group achieving a pain reduction that was clinically meaningful. Improvements in all other PROMs were notably superior in patients of the Rise-uP group. The AI analysis of app usage discerned four usage clusters. Short- to long-term usage, all produced about the same level of pain reduction. Cost-effectiveness analysis indicated a substantial economic benefit for Rise-uP.

rows.duration_additional_info: Adverse Events from 2018 to 2019 were included
rows.collected_data: The most common nonserious AE reported was increased pain. Other nonserious AEs reported included muscle issues, unpleasant sensations, headache, dizziness, and sleep disturbances. One serious AE, a surgery, was reported. Details of the event and its connection to the intervention were not obtainable, as the user did not provide more information when asked to do so; therefore, it was considered to be possibly related to the intervention.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: AEs were most frequently reported by users who had 0 to 99 active days on the app, and less frequently reported by users with more active days on the app. This study on the Kaia App provides the first comprehensive assessment of reported AEs associated with real-world use of digital therapeutics for lower back pain. The overall rate of reported AEs was very low, but significant reporting bias is likely to be present. The AEs reported were generally consistent with those described for in-person therapies for LBP.

Limitations: Small sample, ~20% dropout.

rows.key_outcomes_findings: Primary Outcome (PHQ-9 Depression Severity): Significant reduction in self-reported depression symptoms post-treatment (3 months); clinically meaningful 5-point drop observed in intervention group vs. control. Good reliability (α=0.83). Secondary Outcomes (Clinician-Rated): HDRS-24: Moderate improvement in depression severity (α=0.79); high interrater reliability (r=0.92–0.96). QIDS-C16: Notable symptom reduction (α=0.75); diagnoses via MINI confirmed MDD in most participants. Suicidality: Low incidence; no intervention-related increases. rows.key_outcomes_findings: 32 patients were included in the per-protocol analysis (intervention: n = 64, control: n = 68). Both the ITT and per-protocol analyses revealed a significant intervention effect in the primary outcome measure BIAS-R. This indicates that the use of neolexon® Aphasia  as an adjunct to standard speech therapy led to greater improvements in the patients' language impairments than standard speech therapy alone. Furthermore, significant positive effects of using the digital health application (DiGA) were observed on the secondary outcomes of emotional health and quality of life.

rows.key_outcomes_findings:
The clinical trial was able to show positive outcomes on the KOOS Pain and Symptoms subscale of the primary endpoint KOOS in the interventional group compared to the standard of care group. A statistically significant pain reduction could be seen at t1 (p = 0.0005), t2 (p = 0.0068) and t4 (approximately 6 weeks after surgery; p = 0.01). A significant
reduction of symptoms at t1 (p = 0.0001), t2 (p = 0.0036) and t6 (p = 0.0377) were recognized. These findings are further supported by the significant benefit of the pain NRS at t4 and positive tendencies at t3, t5 and t6. The KOOS subscales quality of life (p = 0.0066), activities of daily living (p = 0.0009) and Sport (p = 0.0484) showed significant effects before
surgery.

Primary outcome:
The two primary endpoints are the comparison of baseline-adjusted follow-up scores between intervention group and control group regarding osteoarthritis-specific pain (Knee Osteoarthritis Outcome Score, Subscale Pain) and physical function (Knee Osteoarthritis Outcome Score, Subscale Activities of daily living) directly after the 12-week intervention phase.

Secondary endpoints include self-reporting on further health outcomes (e.g., health-related quality of life (VR12), fear of movement, other knee-related complaints (Knee Osteoarthritis Outcome Score, other subscales), Patient's Global Assessment). In addition, usability and patient satisfaction will be evaluated.

studies.key_outcomes_findings: The guided ACTonPain group showed significantly less pain interference than the control group in the ITT analysis (p = 0.01), with a moderate effect size at T1 and T2 (d = 0.58 respectively), corresponding to a number needed to treat (NNT) of 3.14 for both time points. Participants in the guided ACTonPain group also indicated higher pain acceptance (T1: d = 0.59; T2: d = 0.76). The unguided ACTonPain group showed to be significantly less depressed in comparison to the control group at at time T2 (d = 0.50). No significant differences with respect to effectiveness were found between the two ACTonPain groups (p>0.05).

studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention: A minimally guided online self-help program based on Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), specifically systematic behavioural activation. Included six core sessions, two optional sessions, and an optional booster session.  Control: An online psychoeducation program on depression.  Participants were adults with Type 1 or Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus and elevated depressive symptoms (CES-D > 22).
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Level of depression (assessed by the German version of the Center for Epidemiological Studies Depression Scale - CES-D).  Secondary Outcomes: Diabetes-specific emotional distress, glycaemic control, self-management behaviour, satisfaction, and costs.  Utility measures for economic evaluation: QALYs (specific instrument not detailed in the economic summary, but standard QALY calculation was used)
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Effectiveness: GET.ON M.E.D. was effective in reducing symptoms of depression over 2 and 6 months compared to the active control group.  Sustained effect: 45% of participants showed no clinically relevant symptoms at 6 months post-randomization. Economic: The intervention has a high probability of being cost-effective compared with the active psychoeducation control group, especially from a cost-effectiveness perspective.  Incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) and incremental cost-utility ratio (ICUR) were calculated using non-parametric bootstrap resampling techniques (2500 replications) over a 6-month period.

studies.collected_data: The present study examined for the first time diabetes patients’ acceptance of Internet-based depression interventions for diabetes patients. Moreover, the impact of an acceptance facilitating intervention (AFI) consisting of an informational session on diabetes patients’ acceptance of IBIs for depression was examined
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Only 11.6% of the participants who showed interest in receiving some form of psychological or behavioral intervention expressed their serious interest in an IBI, while 51.8% negated being interested in this form of intervention. Compared to the other delivery options, IBIs were the least favored intervention approach. Thus, while IBIs are effective for the treatment of depression  and attrition rates of IBI participants are rather low for those who started an IBI , the reach of IBIs in the whole target population seems very limited. Participants of the study conducted by Gun et al. reported that the likelihood to take up an IBI in the future could be facilitated by providing them with information on the effectiveness, accessibility and safety of IBIs; a suggestion that we experimentally tested in the present study.

studies.collected_data: ssessments of pain, psychological state, functional capacity, and well-being (patient-reported outcome measures; PROMs) were collected at baseline, and at 3-, 6-, and 12-months follow-up intervals. Health insurance partners AOK, DAK, and BARMER provided individual healthcare cost data. An artificial intelligence (AI)-driven behavioral tracking analysis identified distinct app usage clusters that presented all with about the same clinical outcome. Patient satisfaction (patient-reported experience measures; PREMs) was captured at the end of the trial.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis demonstrated that the Rise-uP group experienced significantly greater pain reduction at 12 months compared to the control group (IG: − 46% vs CG: − 24%; p < 0.001) with only the Rise-uP group achieving a pain reduction that was clinically meaningful. Improvements in all other PROMs were notably superior in patients of the Rise-uP group. The AI analysis of app usage discerned four usage clusters. Short- to long-term usage, all produced about the same level of pain reduction. Cost-effectiveness analysis indicated a substantial economic benefit for Rise-uP.

studies.duration_additional_info: Adverse Events from 2018 to 2019 were included
studies.collected_data: The most common nonserious AE reported was increased pain. Other nonserious AEs reported included muscle issues, unpleasant sensations, headache, dizziness, and sleep disturbances. One serious AE, a surgery, was reported. Details of the event and its connection to the intervention were not obtainable, as the user did not provide more information when asked to do so; therefore, it was considered to be possibly related to the intervention.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: AEs were most frequently reported by users who had 0 to 99 active days on the app, and less frequently reported by users with more active days on the app. This study on the Kaia App provides the first comprehensive assessment of reported AEs associated with real-world use of digital therapeutics for lower back pain. The overall rate of reported AEs was very low, but significant reporting bias is likely to be present. The AEs reported were generally consistent with those described for in-person therapies for LBP.

Available case analyses: Significant BDI reduction (Cohen's d=0.64, 95% CI 0.33–0.94) and WSA improvement (d=0.64, 95% CI 0.33–0.95) favoring immediate-treatment. Intervention group had higher odds of reliable symptom reduction (OR=6.8, 95% CI 2.90–18.19) and recovery (OR=17.3, 95% CI 2.3–130). Effects maintained at 6-month follow-up. 83% of users rated Deprexis as helpful; average 5.1 modules completed.

studies.key_outcomes_findings:
The clinical trial was able to show positive outcomes on the KOOS Pain and Symptoms subscale of the primary endpoint KOOS in the interventional group compared to the standard of care group. A statistically significant pain reduction could be seen at t1 (p = 0.0005), t2 (p = 0.0068) and t4 (approximately 6 weeks after surgery; p = 0.01). A significant
reduction of symptoms at t1 (p = 0.0001), t2 (p = 0.0036) and t6 (p = 0.0377) were recognized. These findings are further supported by the significant benefit of the pain NRS at t4 and positive tendencies at t3, t5 and t6. The KOOS subscales quality of life (p = 0.0066), activities of daily living (p = 0.0009) and Sport (p = 0.0484) showed significant effects before
surgery.

studies.collected_data: The primary endpoint was improvement in best-corrected visual acuity (logMAR). The secondary endpoint was improvement in stereopsis. Point in time (t0,t1,t2)
studies.key_outcomes_findings: After 3 months, the combination of Caterna plus occlusion achieved 1.4 additional lines of visual acuity compared with occlusion monotherapy (Figure 1). Best-corrected visual acuity improved by 0.15 logMAR (95% CI [0.11, 0.18], p < .001) in the intervention group, compared with 0.01 logMAR (95% CI [-0.02, 0.04], p = .356) in the control group. Stereoscopic vision showed no significant change

studies.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-8) for depression, GAD-7 for anxiety disorders.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: ISI reduced from 18.8 (baseline) to 9.9 at Core 6 completion (p<.001; d=1.7); remained significantly lower at 6-month (11.6) and 1-year (12.2) follow-ups (both p<.001). PHQ-8 decreased from 12.3 to 7.6 at immediate post (p<.001). GAD-7 decreased from 10.0 to 6.9 at immediate post (p<.001), sustained at 1 year (7.0). At post-intervention, 49.2% were treatment responders; 32.3% achieved remission.

studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention: Feel DTx — a 16-week data-driven digital therapeutic combining CBT, mindfulness, biopsychology and positive psychology delivered via mobile app + proprietary Feel Emotion Sensor (FES) wearable + weekly digital health coach check-ins. Control: Waitlist control — no intervention during the 16-week study period; offered access to the intervention after study completion
studies.collected_data: Primary PHQ-9 (depression) and GAD-7 (anxiety) at baseline (w0), mid (w8), post (w16). Secondary SWLS — Satisfaction With Life Scale; LISAT-11 — Life Satisfaction Questionnaire
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group demonstrated significant reductions in both depressive (PHQ-9 mean change = −5.61, CI −7.14 to −4.08) and anxiety symptoms (GAD-7 mean change = −5.21, CI −6.66 to −3.76) compared to the waitlist control, with medium-to-large between-group effect sizes (r=0.64 and r=0.62, p<0.001) at 16 weeks. Secondary outcomes showed significant improvements in quality of life (SWLS: r=0.47; LISAT-11: r=0.59, both p<0.001). Engagement was high, with 77.1% of participants active weekly (98% per protocol), averaging 57.65 minutes/week in-app and completing 7.83 DHIs per week. No adverse events were reported.

studies.key_outcomes_findings: ISI improved from 14.1 (baseline) to 11.2 (intervention, p<.001). PROMIS Sleep Disturbance improved (13.9 vs. 11.5, p<.001). studies.collected_data:
Primary: Oswestry Disability Index (ODI)
Secondary: Numerical Pain Rating Scale (NPRS, 0–10); Readiness for Surgery (0–100); Fear-Avoidance Beliefs Questionnaire – Physical Activity (FABQ-PA, 0–24); GAD-7 (anxiety, 0–21); PHQ-9 (depression, 0–27); IPAQ-SF (physical activity); WPAI (Work Productivity and Activity Impairment: overall, work, time, activity); patient satisfaction (0–10); engagement/adherence (sessions, dropout rate, total time)
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Both groups showed significant improvements in the primary outcome, the ODI (DG: −6.90; CG: −6.35; both p < 0.001), with no significant difference between groups (difference: −0.55; 95% CI: −2.42 to 5.81; p = 0.412; effect size: −0.13) . No significant difference in pain, anxiety, depression, willingness to undergo surgery, or work productivity. Significantly lower dropout rate in the DG (15.7% vs. 34.3%; p=0.019). Completion rate: DG 81.4% vs. CG 64.3%. Satisfaction was high and comparable (IG: 8.4/10; CG: 8.4/10; p=0.837). Only the IG showed a significant increase in physical activity (p=0.008)
